title: Client Side Vocal Separation

comments.author: Komediruzecki
comments.body:
## Client-Side Vocal Separation — Investigation Plan

Here’s the plan, broken into phases to investigate one piece at a time.

---

### 1. Technology Landscape

Three viable paths for browser-based source separation:

| Path | Engine | GPU | CPU Fallback | Maturity |
|------|--------|-----|--------------|----------|
| **A: ONNX Runtime Web** | `onnxruntime-web` + WebGPU | Yes (WebGPU) | Yes (WASM) | Production-ready (v1.20+, 2025) |
| **B: TensorFlow.js** | `@tensorflow/tfjs` + WebGL | Yes (WebGL) | Yes (WASM) | Mature, Spleeter ports exist |
| **C: Custom WASM** | Compiled C inference + handwritten DSP | No | Yes | High effort, fragile |

**Recommendation: Path A — ONNX Runtime Web + WebGPU.** It has the best GPU acceleration story, supports the models UVR already uses (via ONNX export), and has a WASM CPU fallback. WebGPU is shipping in Chrome/Edge (2023+), Firefox (2025), with Safari behind a flag.

---

### 2. Investigation Phases

#### Phase 1 — Model Conversion (1-2 days)
- Export a single UVR model (e.g. MDX-Net or Demucs 2-stem) from PyTorch → ONNX
- Test ONNX model correctness with `onnxruntime` (Python) to validate the export pipeline
- Measure exported model size (expect 50–200MB depending on model)
- **Deliverable**: working ONNX model + export script

#### Phase 2 — Browser Inference Prototype (3-5 days)
- Set up `onnxruntime-web` with WebGPU execution provider
- Implement STFT/ISTFT in JavaScript (or use ONNX ops for the full pipeline)
- Build a minimal page: load ONNX model → feed audio → get separated stems
- Measure: inference time, memory usage, browser compatibility
- **Deliverable**: proof-of-concept running a 2-stem separation on a 30s clip

#### Phase 3 — Model Caching & Delivery (1-2 days)
- Implement IndexedDB caching: download ONNX model once, store as `ArrayBuffer`, load from cache on subsequent visits
- Investigate model sharding (split large models into chunks for progressive download)
- Handle versioning: ETag checks so cached models update when new versions ship
- **Deliverable**: model downloads once, cached indefinitely, updates transparently

#### Phase 4 — Full Song Processing (1-2 days)
- Audio decoding in browser (Web Audio API `decodeAudioData` for MP3/WAV)
- Chunked processing: split audio into overlapping segments, process on GPU, crossfade-stitch results
- Memory management: release GPU buffers between chunks to stay within WebGPU limits
- **Deliverable**: end-to-end processing of a 4-minute song without OOM

#### Phase 5 — Worker Architecture & UI (1-2 days)
- Move inference to a Web Worker to keep the main thread responsive
- Progress reporting: percent complete, estimated time remaining
- Abort/cancel support
- **Deliverable**: non-blocking UX with progress bar

#### Phase 6 — Fallback & Portability (1 day)
- Detect WebGPU support → use it; if not → fall back to ONNX Runtime WASM (CPU)
- Detect model availability: if no cached model, show "Download model (X MB)" prompt
- Test across Chrome, Edge, Firefox, Safari
- **Deliverable**: works on any modern browser, gracefully degrades

---

### 3. Key Questions & Answers

**How feasible is this?**
Feasible but non-trivial. ONNX Runtime Web already supports the core ML ops. The main unknowns are: whether specific UVR model architectures export cleanly to ONNX, and whether WebGPU buffer limits handle the model sizes in practice.

**What are the main drawbacks vs server-side?**
- 3-10x slower than native CUDA (WebGPU is less optimized; browser GPU has tighter memory limits)
- Limited to smaller models (50-200MB range — larger models may hit browser GPU buffer caps at 128-256MB)
- No multi-GPU, no CUDA-specific optimizations (mixed precision, tensor cores)
- First visit requires downloading the model (50-200MB), which is a UX hurdle

**How big are the binaries?**
- `onnxruntime-web` WASM: ~10MB (gzipped ~3MB)
- ONNX model: 50-200MB per model (2-stem vocal/instrumental is typically ~80MB)
- Your app JS/CSS: already ~500KB
- Total first-visit payload: 60-210MB depending on model choice
- Subsequent visits: only ~3MB (WASM from cache) + model from IndexedDB (zero network)

**How to support CPU/GPU?**
- **GPU**: WebGPU → ONNX Runtime WebGPU EP. Works on integrated/discrete GPUs from NVIDIA, AMD, Intel, Apple Silicon. The browser abstracts the GPU vendor — no CUDA/ROCm/Metal code needed.
- **CPU**: ONNX Runtime WASM EP with multi-threading. ~3-10x slower than GPU but always available.
- Detection: `navigator.gpu` → if available, use WebGPU; otherwise fall back to WASM.

**Can models be cached?**
Yes. IndexedDB can store files up to ~60% of disk space (Chromium), 1-2GB per origin. A Service Worker + Cache API is an alternative for smaller models. The ONNX Runtime `InferenceSession.create()` accepts raw `ArrayBuffer`, so plugging in IndexedDB is straightforward.

---

### 4. Summary

| Aspect | Assessment |
|--------|-----------|
| **Feasibility** | Feasible — ONNX Runtime Web is production-ready |
| **Difficulty** | Medium-Hard (6/10) — model export is the riskiest step |
| **Effort** | 8-14 days for MVP (2-stem separation) |
| **Main risk** | UVR model architecture may not export cleanly to ONNX |
| **Best-case outcome** | Client-side 2-stem separation, ~30-60s per song on GPU, ~3-10min on CPU |
| **Worst-case outcome** | Model export fails for key architectures → fall back to server-side only |

---

### 5. Next Step

I’ll write the detailed investigation doc to `docs/plans/client-side-vocal-separation.md` covering the architecture, model conversion pipeline, caching strategy, and fallback matrix. Then we tackle Phase 1 (model export) as the first concrete action.

Want me to go ahead and write that doc?

author: Komediruzecki
body:
## Feature: Synthesia-Style Falling Notes Piano Practice

Add a new "Piano Practice" tab where users play their piano into the microphone and see Synthesia-style falling notes they must play in time.

### How It Works
- **Vertical falling notes**: Notes fall from top toward a horizontal judgment line above a virtual piano keyboard at the bottom
- **Mic input**: Reuses existing `PitchDetector` to identify which note the user played on their piano
- **Hit detection**: When a falling note crosses the judgment line, check if the user played the correct note within timing windows
- **Scoring**: Dual-axis scoring — timing accuracy (perfect/great/good/miss) × pitch accuracy (perfect/excellent/good/okay/off)
- **Virtual keyboard**: Shows the piano keyboard at the bottom, highlighting expected keys
- **Song selection**: Load any melody from the library, or import MIDI files

### Architecture
- **New tab**: `'falling-notes'` added to `ActiveTab` union type in `ui-store.ts`
- **New canvas**: `FallingNotesCanvas.tsx` — HTML Canvas 2D, same DPR/ResizeObserver/RAF pattern as `PitchCanvas.tsx`
- **Pitch detection**: Reuses `PitchDetector` + `AudioEngine` directly (lightweight `FallingNotesEngine` wrapper)
- **Song data**: Reuses `MelodyData` format — songs are loaded from the melody library via `melodyStore.loadMelody()`
- **MIDI import**: Reuses `importMelodyFromMIDI()` from `piano-roll.ts`
- **Scoring**: Reuses `centsToRating()`, `ratingToScore()`, `scoreGrade()` from `practice-engine.ts`

### New Files
- `src/stores/falling-notes-store.ts` — Game state signals
- `src/lib/falling-notes-engine.ts` — Mic + pitch adapter
- `src/components/FallingNotesCanvas.tsx` — Main canvas
- `src/features/falling-notes/useFallingNotesController.ts` — Game logic
- `src/components/FallingNotesSongPicker.tsx` — Song selector
- `src/styles/falling-notes.css` — Layout styles

### Modified Files
- `src/stores/ui-store.ts` — Add tab type
- `src/App.tsx` — Tab button + content block + controller wiring
- Barrel exports in `stores/index.ts`, `components/index.ts`, `lib/index.ts`

### Implementation Phases
1. Foundation: store, engine, tab type
2. Canvas: falling notes rendering, keyboard, judgment line
3. Game logic: RAF loop, hit detection, scoring
4. Song picker: library browser + MIDI import
5. Integration: wire into App.tsx
6. Polish: particles, animations, score overlay

### Design Decisions
- Vertical scroll (Synthesia-style) — keyboard is horizontal, notes approach the keyboard
- Direct PitchDetector reuse — no full PracticeEngine lifecycle needed
- Separate store — different state needs than practice sessions
- MelodyData as song format — no new data format needed

See plan file for full implementation details.
title: Falling Notes Piano Practice Feature

author: Komediruzecki
body:
## Summary

Need to implement a complete UVR (Ultimate Vocal Remover) workflow in PitchPerfect:

1. **Import MP3/WAV** - Allow users to upload audio files
2. **UVR Processing** - Send audio to UVR for stem separation (vocals/instrumental)
3. **Stem Output** - Provide separated vocal and instrumental stems
4. **MIDI Generation** - Run pitch detection on vocal stem and create MIDI melody
5. **Practice Modes** - Practice with:
   - MIDI melody only
   - Backing track only
   - Full mix (karaoke-style)

## Current State

The app has a `UvrSettings` UI component with sliders, but these are not connected to any backend logic. The settings save to localStorage but don't actually process audio.

## Requirements

### 1. File Upload UI
- Support MP3 and WAV formats
- Preview audio before processing
- Show file info (duration, size)

### 2. UVR Integration
- Use the UVR CLI from `Anjok07/ultimatevocalremovergui`
- Options: VR Architecture (Model_AHB), model selection (UVR_MDXNET_model_config)
- Output: stems as WAV files

### 3. Stem Processing
- After UVR processing, have:
  - `vocal-stem.wav` - cleaned vocals
  - `instrumental-stem.wav` - background music

### 4. Pitch Detection & MIDI
- Process `vocal-stem.wav` with pitch detector
- Create MIDI melody from detected notes
- Support tempo detection and note quantization

### 5. Practice Integration
- Practice session uses generated MIDI or stems
- Allow user to select practice mode (MIDI only, backings only, full mix)

## Technical Approach

**Option 1: Server-side Processing** (Recommended)
- Upload MP3 to server
- Server calls UVR CLI
- Return stems + MIDI as response
- Fast, reliable, no client resource limits

**Option 2: Client-side WASM**
- Build UVR CLI to WASM
- Process locally
- More complex, larger bundle

## Questions

1. Should the UVR processing be server-side or client-side?
2. What's the expected workflow for users?
3. Should multiple stem configurations be available?
4. How to handle large audio files?

## Related

- UVR GitHub: https://github.com/Anjok07/ultimatevocalremovergui
title: [Feature] Full UVR Workflow - Import, Split Stems, Generate MIDI

## Proposed Implementation Plan

### Architecture Overview
Implement a pluggable framework for comparing pitch detection algorithms with accuracy scoring, performance metrics, and a dedicated debug/testing UI.

---

### Tasks

1. **Algorithm Data Structure**
   - Extend `PitchAlgorithm` type with algorithm metadata
   - Define default algorithm list (YIN, AutoCorrelate, etc.)
   - Each algorithm tracks: name, description, computation time, accuracy

2. **Sample Test Dataset**
   - Create synthetic dataset with known frequencies (+/- cents variations)
   - Include octave ranges, perfect intervals, and in-tune notes
   - Save for programmatic reuse

3. **Accuracy Scoring Engine**
   - For each algorithm, calculate offset from ground truth
   - Score out of 100% based on within +/- 10 cents, +/- 25 cents
   - Measure per-sample computation time

4. **Test Runner UI Component**
   - dedicated panel for algorithm comparison
   - Load sample → Run all algorithms side-by-side
   - Display results table with scores and timing

5. **Pluggable Framework**
   - Clear interface for adding new algorithms
   - Runtime algorithm selection/registration

6. **Performance Metrics**
   - Track CPU, memory, frame time for each algorithm
   - Evaluate realtime viability (60fps target)

---

### Proposed File Structure
- `src/lib/pitch-algorithm-tester.ts` - Core testing engine
- `src/lib/pitch-algorithms.ts` - Algorithm registry and metadata
- `src/components/PitchAlgorithmTester.tsx` - UI component
- `src/data/pitch-test-samples.ts` - Test dataset

comments.author: Komediruzecki
comments.body:
## Implementation Plan: UVR Integration

### Overview
Integrate Ultravox AI (UVR5) for music source separation to extract vocals from imported music tracks, then map those vocals to melody notes for pitch practice.

### Technical Options

**Option A: Node.js CLI (easiest for quick integration)**
- Deploy UVR5 as a Docker container or install locally
- Use the Node.js CLI via `child_process` for track separation
- Pros: Simple API, no WASM learning curve
- Cons: Requires separate process, slower than WASM, needs container orchestration

**Option B: WASM Build**
- Compile UVR to WebAssembly (if available)
- Load UVR Wasm files from CDN
- Pros: Faster, no external process, fully client-side
- Cons: Complex setup, learning curve, bundle size

### Recommended Approach

Start with **Option A (Node.js CLI)** for the MVP, with a clear path to WASM:

**Phase 1: Backend Service**
1. Create  endpoint
2. Containerize UVR5 with Docker
3. Accept uploaded audio files → send to UVR → return vocal-only track
4. Use Flask/FastAPI wrapper (lightweight) for this

**Phase 2: Melody Mapping**
1. Convert extracted vocal track to MIDI using our existing `.processToMelody()` 
2. Parse MIDI to melody notes (same logic as built-in melodies)
3. Return both raw audio + mapped melody for practice

**Phase 3: Cleanup & Optimization** (Future)
- Explore WASM version for client-side-only workflow
- Optimize for performance with progress streaming

### API Design

```typescript
interface VocalSeparationRequest {
  audioFile: File | Buffer
  model: 'VRArch' | 'MDX-Net' | 'ConvTasNet'
  returnMelody?: boolean  // Return also processed melody
}

interface VocalSeparationResponse {
  vocalTrack: Buffer  // Processed vocal-only audio
  melody?: MelodyData  // Optional mapped melody
  duration: number
  sampleRate: number
}
```

### Implementation Details

1. **Storage**: Store temporary uploaded files in `/tmp/pp-uploads/` with cleanup cron
2. **Queue**: Use Bull/Redis or in-memory queue for processing (UVR is CPU intensive)
3. **Progress**: SSE (Server-Sent Events) for long-running jobs
4. **Models**: Pre-install popular models on container: VRArch, MDX-Net

### Next Steps
1. Containerize UVR5 and test separation quality
2. Implement the Node.js CLI wrapper
3. Build the API endpoint
4. Integrate with VocalAnalysis tab for "Separate Vocal from Music" workflow

body:
## Problem
Quick Start recursion in handleSessionSkip() can cause performance issues with large session durations (many rest items or long rest durations).

## Context
When a session contains rest items with long durations, the recursive setTimeout pattern can lead to excessive delays:

```typescript
const handleSessionSkip = () => {
  // ... skip current item ...
  setTimeout(handleSessionSkip, duration)
}
```

For sessions with 10+ rest items or rest durations >30 seconds, this causes:
- Cumulative delays approaching server timeout limits
- Poor user experience during practice
- Risk of hitting React render limits

## Current Implementation
Found in `src/components/SessionPlayer.tsx`:
- Recursively calls `handleSessionSkip` via setTimeout for each rest item
- No timeout cap or cancellation mechanism

## Suggested Fix Options
1. **Replace recursion with proper lifecycle management** using SolidJS `createEffect` or `requestAnimationFrame`
2. **Add timeout ceiling** (e.g., max 60-90s per rest)
3. **Use async/await with delay generator** instead of recursion
4. **Batch processing** for long sequences

## Requirements
- Maintain existing session playback behavior
- Handle sessions with 20+ rest items smoothly
- Prevent timeout issues during practice
- Respect user's cancel/stop session actions
title: Fix: Quick Start recursion with rest items causing timeouts

author: Komediruzecki
body:
You are a senior frontend engineer performing a SAFE, CONSISTENT naming refactor across a TypeScript + SolidJS codebase.

You have full access to the project and can modify files.

The prompt should be chain executed together with: https://github.com/Komediruzecki/pitch-perfect/issues/177,
“Use your findings from previous analysis to drive the renaming strategy.”

---

## 🎯 GOAL

Improve naming across the entire codebase to be:
- consistent
- descriptive
- domain-aligned
- idiomatic for SolidJS + TypeScript

WITHOUT breaking functionality.

---

## 📏 NAMING RULES (ENFORCE STRICTLY)

### General
- Use camelCase for variables and functions
- Use PascalCase for components
- Use UPPER_CASE for constants
- Avoid abbreviations unless standard (id, url)

---

### SolidJS-specific
- Signals:
  const [count, setCount]
  NOT:
  const [value, setValue]

- Derived values:
  use clear names:
  totalBalance (NOT calc1)

- Event handlers:
  handleClick, handleSubmit

---

### Domain Naming
- Use full semantic names:
  txn → transaction
  acc → account
  bal → balance

---

## 🔍 PROCESS

### 1. Identify rename candidates
- unclear variables
- inconsistent naming
- duplicates with different names

---

### 2. Build rename map

Example:
{
  "bal": "balance",
  "txnList": "transactions",
  "val": "value" (or better replacement)
}

---

### 3. Apply refactor SAFELY

You MUST:
- Update all references (including imports/exports)
- Respect scope
- Avoid renaming public APIs unless consistent across project
- Avoid breaking type definitions

---

### 4. SolidJS Reactivity Safety

Ensure:
- Signals still work correctly
- No destructuring that breaks reactivity
- No stale references

---

### 5. File Naming (if needed)
- Rename files to match components
- Ensure imports updated

---

## 🧪 VALIDATION (MANDATORY)

After refactor, run:

- type check (tsc)
- lint
- tests

Fix any issues introduced.

---

## 📦 OUTPUT

### ✅ Summary
- What was renamed
- Why

### 🔁 Rename Map (full)

### ⚠️ Edge Cases
- Anything uncertain or risky

---

## 🚫 DO NOT

- Change logic
- Refactor structure (only naming)
- Introduce new patterns

---

## 🎯 END GOAL

A clean, consistent naming system across the entire codebase with zero broken functionality.
title: Project overview chain and refactor

author: Komediruzecki
body:
You are a senior frontend architect and codebase analyst.

Your task is to analyze a TypeScript + SolidJS + Vite project and produce a VISUAL and STRUCTURAL overview of the entire codebase, with a strong focus on naming, scope, and architecture clarity.

You can execute commands, read files, and traverse the project.

---

## 🎯 GOALS

1. Build a mental model of the entire project
2. Visualize structure (files, modules, components, dependencies)
3. Analyze naming conventions and consistency
4. Classify variables by scope and purpose
5. Identify confusing, misleading, or inconsistent naming

---

## 🗺️ PROJECT STRUCTURE MAPPING

Produce a clear structural overview:

### 1. File Tree (cleaned)
- Collapse irrelevant folders (node_modules, dist, etc.)
- Highlight:
  - components/
  - pages/
  - stores/
  - utils/
  - styles/

### 2. Dependency Graph (HIGH LEVEL)
Show relationships:
- Which components import which
- Shared utilities
- State/store usage

Use a readable format like:
ComponentA
  ├── uses: utilX, storeY
  └── renders: ComponentB

---

## 🧠 NAMING ANALYSIS

### 3. Naming Convention Detection
Identify patterns used:
- camelCase
- kebab-case (CSS)
- PascalCase (components)
- inconsistencies

---

### 4. Variable Classification

For each significant file, classify variables:

#### 🟢 Local variables
- inside functions/components

#### 🔵 Component state
- signals (`createSignal`)
- memos (`createMemo`)

#### 🟣 Derived/computed values

#### 🟠 Global/shared
- stores
- module-level variables

#### 🔴 Imports
- external libs
- internal modules

---

### 5. Naming Quality Audit

Detect and list:

#### 🚨 Problems
- unclear names (`data`, `value`, `temp`, `x`)
- misleading names
- inconsistent abbreviations
- same concept named differently across files
- overly long or overly short names

#### 🧩 Collisions
- same name used for different meanings
- different names used for same meaning

---

### 6. Semantic Grouping

Group variables by domain concepts:
(e.g. finance app)
- transactions
- balances
- charts
- filters

Check if naming reflects domain properly.

---

## 🎨 VISUALIZATION OUTPUT

Provide:

### 🗂 Project Overview (Tree)

### 🔗 Dependency Graph (text-based)

### 🧠 Naming Map

Example:

balance:

balance
totalBalance
accountBalance
bal (❌ inconsistent)

---

### 📊 Consistency Score
Rate:
- Naming consistency (0–100)
- Structure clarity (0–100)

---

### 🚨 Critical Issues
Top naming/structure problems

---

### 🧭 Refactor Strategy (HIGH LEVEL ONLY)
DO NOT rename yet.

Instead:
- Define naming rules
- Define conventions
- Suggest patterns to standardize

---

## ⚠️ RULES

- Be precise, not generic
- Prefer concrete examples from code
- Do not modify code in this step
- Focus on clarity, not verbosity

---

## 🎯 END GOAL

Produce a clear, visual mental model of the project and expose all naming inconsistencies before refactoring begins.
title: Project overview map

author: Komediruzecki
body:
You are a senior frontend engineer specializing in migrations to SolidJS. 
Your task is to audit a codebase that is being migrated from plain HTML/CSS/JavaScript to SolidJS.

Your goal is to deeply analyze the current state of the migration and identify:
- What is correctly migrated
- What is partially migrated or inconsistent
- What is broken, risky, or incorrectly implemented
- What is still legacy (plain JS/DOM manipulation) and should be refactored

You must act systematically and verify the project like a CI pipeline + code reviewer combined.

---

## 🧭 CONTEXT

The project is a frontend web application (finance-related UI) originally built using:
- Plain HTML
- CSS
- Vanilla JavaScript (DOM manipulation, event listeners, imperative logic)

It is being migrated to:
- SolidJS
- TypeScript (if present)
- Modern tooling (Vite, ESLint, Prettier, testing frameworks)

---

## 🧪 YOUR TASKS

### 1. Project Structure & Tooling Audit
- Detect build system (Vite, Webpack, etc.)
- Verify SolidJS setup correctness
- Check tsconfig (if TypeScript is used)
- Validate ESLint + Prettier configuration
- Ensure scripts exist and work:
  - `dev`
  - `build`
  - `lint`
  - `format`
  - `test`

Flag:
- Missing scripts
- Misconfigured paths
- Incorrect plugin usage
- Broken imports or aliases

---

### 2. Lint / Format / Type Check Execution
Ruu:
- ESLint
- Prettier
- TypeScript (tsc)
- fmt 
- etc...

Report:
- Errors
- Warnings
- Inconsistencies
- Files ignored unintentionally

---

### 3. SolidJS Migration Quality Check

#### 🚨 Detect anti-patterns:
- Direct DOM manipulation (`document.querySelector`, etc.)
- Manual event listeners instead of JSX handlers
- State managed outside Solid primitives
- Global mutable state without reactivity

#### ✅ Verify correct Solid usage:
- `createSignal`, `createEffect`, `createMemo`
- Component structure
- Props handling
- JSX usage instead of string templates
- Proper reactivity (no stale closures, no unnecessary recomputation)

---

### 4. Reactivity & State Integrity
Check for:
- Missing signals where state should be reactive
- Overuse of signals where not needed
- Incorrect destructuring breaking reactivity
- Derived values not using `createMemo`

---

### 5. CSS & Styling Migration
- Detect legacy global CSS issues
- Check for:
  - CSS modules usage
  - Naming inconsistencies (kebab-case vs camelCase)
- Identify unused styles
- Ensure styles match new component structure

---

### 6. Componentization Audit
- Identify large monolithic files that should be components
- Detect duplicated UI logic
- Suggest component boundaries

---

### 7. Testing Coverage
- Detect testing framework (Vitest, Jest, etc.)
- Check:
  - Are tests present?
  - Are they passing?
  - Are components tested or only utilities?

Flag:
- Missing tests for critical UI logic
- Broken or outdated tests

---

### 8. Performance & UX Risks
- Detect unnecessary re-renders
- Identify inefficient computations
- Spot synchronous heavy operations in UI

---

### 9. Migration Completeness Report

Classify files into:
- ✅ Fully migrated
- ⚠️ Partially migrated
- ❌ Not migrated (still legacy)

---

### 10. Output Format

Produce:

#### 🔍 Summary
- Overall migration completeness (% estimate)
- Major risks
- Readiness for production (Yes/No + why)

#### 📂 Detailed Findings
Grouped by:
- Tooling
- Code quality
- SolidJS usage
- Styling
- Tests

#### 🧨 Critical Issues (must fix first)
Bullet list with severity

#### 🛠 Suggested Fixes
Concrete actionable steps (not vague advice)

#### 🧱 Refactor Suggestions
- Component splits
- State restructuring
- File organization

---

## ⚠️ IMPORTANT RULES

- Do NOT assume things work — verify logically
- Be critical, not polite
- Prefer concrete examples over general advice
- Point to exact patterns or code smells
- If unsure, mark as "needs verification"

---

## 🎯 END GOAL

Give a brutally honest, technically precise evaluation of the migration state and a clear path to a clean, idiomatic SolidJS codebase.
title: Check refactor made by porting all js/css/html to vite solidJS frontend

author: Komediruzecki
body:
## Problem

The Editor and Practice tabs have duplicated control toolbar code with overlapping functionality:
- Both use Play/Pause/Continue/Stop buttons  
- Both have volume, speed, and metronome controls
- Editor toolbar doesn't actually control audio playback (broken)

## Current State

**PracticeTabHeader.tsx**: Contains practice-specific controls (modes, cycles, count-in, sensitivity, sessions button)

**EditorTabHeader.tsx**: Contains editor-specific controls (record to piano roll, volume, speed, metronome)

Both have the same core controls but implemented separately.

## Proposed Solution

Create a shared `SharedControlToolbar` component that:
1. Provides all core controls (play, pause, stop, mic, wave, volume, speed, metronome)
2. Uses conditional rendering for tab-specific controls
3. Properly connects to PlaybackRuntime for actual audio playback

## Expected Outcome

- Single source of truth for control toolbar UI
- Editor toolbar will properly control playback audio
- Easier to maintain and extend controls
- Reduces code duplication

See `EDITOR-PLAYBACK-BUGS.md` in the repo for detailed investigation of the playback issues.
title: Refactor: Create shared control toolbar for Editor and Practice tabs
